explicit_text: I've worked for Armour and Company for about two years, though not steadily as I've been laid off twice. At Armour's, I did many jobs like packing brains, peeling vegetables in the soup room, and running tractors hauling finished products. The work was tough, especially in the cold rooms and with hot meat, but I stuck with it because I was a union member and wanted to keep my job. Armour's management was always watching union men closely, and I saw how they tried to push us around, but I kept my place as a tractor man to avoid too much responsibility and scrutiny.
implicit_text: I've worked at a major meatpacking plant for about two years, though not steadily as I've been laid off twice. At this plant, I did many jobs like packing brains, peeling vegetables in the soup room, and running tractors hauling finished products. The work was tough, especially in the cold rooms and with hot meat, but I stuck with it because I was a union member and wanted to keep my job. Management there was always watching union men closely, and I saw how they tried to push us around, but I kept my place as a tractor man to avoid too much responsibility and scrutiny.

explicit_text: I was part of the CIO union, and it was a big deal for us, especially the colored girls who jumped at the chance to join. The company tried to get on our nerves by putting me and another union girl on the vinegar job, which was nasty and caused skin rashes, hoping we'd quit. But we held strong, and once the Polish and Mexican women got involved, they fought hard for their rights and the union. The CIO gave us a sense of power and community in a tough workplace like Armour's.

This union gave us a sense of power and community in a tough workplace like the meatpacking plant.

explicit_text: I worked at Wilson's sausage department, which was a wet and greasy place where the meat was put into stuffers and then linked into sausages by women on both sides of the table. The sausages were heavy, and women had to carry full cages weighing up to 200 pounds, which was really hard work. I remember how tough it was, with cuts on my fingers and the strain of handling such heavy loads. It was a demanding job, but I did it for a while alongside other women, including a Negro woman who had to lug those heavy cages around.

explicit_text: On the pickled pigs feet job, we had to carry jars on our fingers, sometimes five at a time, and it looked like something out of Buck Rogers with all the glass jars stuck on our fingers. The tops of the jars were sharp and jagged, and we often cut ourselves, especially when the vinegar splashed on our wounds. It was a tough and painful job, and the image of us rushing past each other with jars on our fingers reminded me of those futuristic Buck Rogers creations.
